data.title: Eurozone Inflation Holds Steady, Reinforcing ECB Rate Hold Views
data.description:
By Ed Frankl

The eurozone's annual rate of inflation was steady at the European Central Bank's target in July, a surprise likely to underpin expectations that policymakers will leave their key interest unchanged next month.

Consumer prices were 2.0% higher than the same month of last year, matching the inflation rate of June, European Union data agency Eurostat said Friday. That was a little stronger than consensus expectations from economists polled by The Wall Street Journal for inflation to cool to 1.9%.

The steady price growth in July comes after economic growth in the 20-nation eurozone economy has held better than expected. Economic activity increased in the second quarter, counter to expectations that it would contract slightly.

U.S. import duties had been expected to significantly dampen Europe's economic activity in recent months. In the first quarter of the year, the economy was lifted as U.S. firms built stocks of imported goods ahead of President Trump's tariff announcements in early April.

That second-quarter resilience prompted Barclays economists to join others in saying they no longer expect an ECB interest-rate cut in September, according to a note to clients on Thursday.

The Frankfurt-based central bank kept its key rate on hold at 2% last week after eight interest-rate cuts since June 2024, the reversal of its cycle of rate hikes to combat spiralling inflation after the pandemic and Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine.

Eurozone inflation has been at or below the ECB's target now since May, with bank staff forecasts anticipating it will ease to 1.6% on average in 2026 but return to target in 2027.

Its President Christine Lagarde said at last week's monetary-policy meeting that the bank was in "a good place" regarding inflation, and said they would not respond to "some minor deviation" from the target such as the expected 2026 undershoot.

Economists believe U.S. tariffs will dampen inflation in the eurozone by demand for goods made in Europe, while also prompting Chinese businesses to cut their prices as they search for alternatives to a U.S. market where they face sharply higher tariffs.

The appreciation of the euro could also lead to lower inflation, given they make imports into the eurozone cheaper, a prospect that prompted France's central bank governor Francois Villeroy de Galhau to warn that the ECB must be open to lowering rates.

While the trade agreement announced on Sunday between the U.S. and EU raises tariff barriers well beyond what they were at the start of the year, they also lessen market uncertainty, likely helping allow businesses to plan and invest and maintaining some resilience in activity.

Write to Ed Frankl at edward.frankl@wsj.com

data.title: Dow Jones Top Markets Headlines at 5 AM ET: Global Markets Drop After Trump Moves to Raise Tariffs on Scores of Nations | Trump ...
data.description:
Global Markets Drop After Trump Moves to Raise Tariffs on Scores of Nations

U.S. stock futures pointed to a lower open and all European equities markets were down. Asia-Pacific stock markets fell broadly.

----

Trump hits dozens more countries with higher tariffs that start Aug. 7

President Donald Trump late Thursday stuck to his Friday deadline for hitting Canada with higher tariffs, but he gave a one-week delay to other trading partners that had received letters from him, while also announcing steeper levies for dozens of other countries.

----

Big Tech's $400 Billion AI Spending Spree Just Got Wall Street's Blessing

Microsoft joined Nvidia in the $4 trillion market-cap club, while Meta is closing in on the $2 trillion mark.

----

Asia Manufacturing Outlook Marks Five-Year Low as Tariff Concerns Linger

July's S&P manufacturing PMI Future Output Index-which measures expectations regarding output in the year ahead- showed subdued confidence among goods producers in Asia.

----

China's Manufacturing Activity Tumbles Into Contraction

A private gauge of China's manufacturing activity returned to contractionary territory in July, as softening new business growth led factories to scale back production.

----

South Korea's Exports Accelerate, Fueled by Chip Demand

Export growth was stronger than expected in July, driven by strong demand for high-performance computing chips amid an AI boom.

----

Stocks Keep Fighting a Hawkish Fed. Why That Has Worked.

Hawkish commentary from the central bank isn't derailing the market.

----

Treasuries Typically Rally Before a Long-Awaited Fed Rate Cut. Trading on That Is Still a Gamble.

Treasury yield declines can signal whether the Fed will cut interest rates. But where they land after a cut is another story.

----

Trump's Tariffs Questioned by Appeals Court

Judges voice skepticism that the president can use emergency powers to replace the import schedule Congress adopted.

----

U.S.-Canada Trade Talks Dealing With Aluminum-Tariff Levels, Bessent Says

The Treasury secretary's comments signaled possible relief for Canada's aluminum sector.

----

Canadian Economy Seen Picking Up Slightly After Contracting in May

National statistics agency's advance estimate suggests the economy grew just 0.1% on an annualized basis in the second quarter.

----

Trump's Russia Sanctions Strategy Runs Hot and Cold

The president's tighter deadline to reach peace with Ukraine and his threat against Russia's energy customers contrast with a lighter approach to Russia elsewhere.

----

Core PCE Inflation Runs a Touch Hot, Dimming Hopes for September Rate Cut

The Fed's preferred inflation gauge, the personal consumption expenditures price index, is due on Thursday.

----

Jobless claims stay low as layoff activity remains subdued

The data indicate a stable labor market with low layoff activity.

----

Major Banks Nudge Oil Forecast Higher But U.S. Tariffs, OPEC+ Hikes Weigh on Outlook

Major Wall Street banks have modestly lifted their oil-price forecasts for this year and next, but lingering uncertainty over U.S. tariffs and rising OPEC+ output continue to cloud the outlook.

----

Tariffs, Economic Concerns Drag Down Stocks

The S&P 500 fell for the third straight session despite strong earnings from Meta and Microsoft.

data.title: Dow Jones Top Company Headlines at 5 AM ET: Nintendo Gets Profit Boost From Strong Switch 2 Sales | IAG ...
data.description:
Nintendo Gets Profit Boost From Strong Switch 2 Sales

Nintendo's net profit jumped in the first quarter, driven in part by strong sales of its new Switch 2 console.

----

IAG Operating Profit Beats Consensus on Strong Revenue Growth

The British Airways owner said however that it expects underlying costs to increase due to lower capacity growth and higher resilience costs.

----

Apple's iPhone Sales Blow Past Estimates as Customers Raced to Beat Tariffs

The company's revenue was about 10% higher than the same period a year ago, showing an unexpected ability to weather tariff blows.

----

Amazon Shares Fall Because Cloud Unit's Growth Wasn't Enough for Wall Street

The e-commerce giant's quarterly sales results beat expectations, but shares dropped 7% because its cloud computing arm's growth wasn't as strong as rivals'.

----

Why Ford's Made-in-America Strategy Hurts It in Trump's Trade War

The company says new tariff deals with Japan, the EU and South Korea put it at a disadvantage.

----

Berkshire Stock Sees Weird Trading. The A Shares Pop While B Shares Dip.

It is unclear what caused the late-day buying in the A shares, but it looks like it will reverse Friday.

----

Axiata to Start Sale Process For World's Sixth-Largest Tower Company

Malaysian telecom giant Axiata Group is expected to start the sale process this month to sell a majority stake in the world's sixth-largest telecommunications tower company, people familiar with the process said.

----

Bayer Ups Guidance After Pharmaceuticals Unit Outperforms

The German pharmaceutical and agricultural conglomerate said it now expects to post revenue of more than $52.51 billion for the year.

----

Wells Fargo to Name CEO Charlie Scharf Chairman

The appointment rewards the executive who led the bank back from a scandal involving the creation of millions of fake customer accounts.

----

UnitedHealth Group Replaces CFO John Rex

The insurance giant names Wayne DeVeydt as its new finance chief amid a turnaround effort.

----

Trump Talks Tough on Drug Prices but His Demands Have Softened

The president threatened to "deploy every tool" in the administration's arsenal if drug companies don't cut certain prices by late September.

----

Coinbase's Profit Jumps on Crypto Investment Gains

The largest U.S. crypto exchange's revenue fell short of Wall Street expectations.

----

Chevron Is On a Roll Ahead of Second-Quarter Earnings

The energy explorer just closed the acquisition of Hess, and got permission from the Trump administration to resume operations in Venezuela.

----

Paramount Streaming Revenue Grows, While TV Challenges Continue

The media company is set to merge with Skydance Global next week.

----

Reddit Swings to Second-Quarter Profit as AI-Driven Improvements Boost Ad Revenue

Reddit's AI-driven advertisements have helped the company draw in more business, pushing revenue up 78% to $499.6 million, said CFO Drew Vollero.

----

MicroStrategy Swings to Profit on Bitcoin Gains

The company's bitcoin per share increased by 25% in the second quarter from a year earlier.

----

Figma Shares Jump 250% in Their Stock-Market Debut

The software company adds fuel to an IPO comeback already under way.

data.title: REG - Genus - Total Voting Rights
data.description:
RNS Number : 6192T Genus PLC 01 August 2025  

1 August 2025

Genus plc

Total Voting Rights

Genus plc announces that, as at 31 July 2025, its issued share capital consists of 66,037,454 ordinary shares of 10p each with voting rights ('Ordinary Shares'). No Ordinary Shares are currently held in treasury and the voting rights of all the Ordinary Shares are identical.

The above figure may be used by shareholders as the denominator for the calculation by which they will determine if they are required to notify their interest in, or a change to their interest in, Genus under the FCA's Disclosure Guidance and Transparency Rules.

For further information please contact:

Genus:

Lucie Grant, Group General Counsel & Company Secretary                          Tel: +44 (0)1256 347100

Terms and conditions relating to the use and distribution of this information may apply. For further information, please contact rns@lseg.com or visit www.rns.com.

RNS may use your IP address to confirm compliance with the terms and conditions, to analyse how you engage with the information contained in this communication, and to share such analysis on an anonymised basis with others as part of our commercial services. For further information about how RNS and the London Stock Exchange use the personal data you provide us, please see our Privacy Policy.  END  TVRFIFIATEILIIE

data.title: REG - SThree plc - Total Voting Rights
data.description:
RNS Number : 6191T SThree plc 01 August 2025  

1 August 2025

SThree plc

Total Voting Rights

In conformity with the Financial Conduct Authority's Disclosure Guidance and Transparency Rule 5.6.1R, SThree plc (the 'Company') hereby notifies the market of the following:

The Company's issued share capital as of 31 July 2025 consisted of 127,827,523 ordinary shares of 1 pence each ('Ordinary Shares'), of which 35,767 Ordinary Shares were held in Treasury.

Therefore, the total number of voting rights in the Company is 127,791,756. This figure may be used by shareholders as the denominator for the calculations by which they will determine if they are required to notify their interest in, or a change to their interest in, the Company under the Financial Conduct Authority's Disclosure Guidance and Transparency Rules.

Veska Zhelezova

Company Secretarial Assistant

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0906 GMT - Campari delivered reassuring results, helped by its strategy to reduce costs, AlphaValue analyst Filippo Ercole Piva writes in a research note. The Italian distiller booked sales of 1.53 billion euros for the first half, up 0.1% organically on year and meeting Visible Alpha consensus estimates. "The company is demonstrating the strength of its portfolio and its ability to gain market share in strategic countries," the analyst says. Shares are up 7.6% at 6.52 euros. (andrea.figueras@wsj.com)

0901 GMT - The Swiss franc falls to a 5-week low against the U.S. dollar after President Trump announced 39% tariffs on Swiss exports to the U.S., due to come into effect from August 7. This is higher than the 31% which Trump had threatened in April. The franc also weakens against the euro as concerns about the economic impact of tariffs outweigh the Swiss currency's safe-haven status. The U.S. dollar rises 0.5% to a high of 0.8169 francs, LSEG data show. The euro rises 0.5% to 0.9318 francs. Concerns about tariffs are now overtaking the previous tech-driven optimism on markets, Tickmill Group's Patrick Munnelly says in a note. Switzerland's SMI stock-market index falls 0.8%. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0839 GMT - Consumer-goods manufacturer Unilever Indonesia could gain, as its gradual earnings improvement and a likely rise in dividend payouts could attract institutional investors, CGS International analysts write in a note. The analysts expect Unilever Indonesia to declare a one-time special dividend after it spins off its ice-cream business by end-2025, taking the company's overall dividend yield to 13% until 1H 2026. The company's food segment revenue and overall gross margin appear to be stabilizing, they add. They are more confident that the worst of a boycott, linked to conflict in the Middle East, on Unilever Indonesia's goods has passed. CGS raises its rating to add from reduce and lifts its target price for the stock to IDR1,910 from IDR1,170. Shares are 2.3% higher at IDR1,750. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

0836 GMT - Amazon.com shares fall Friday premarket as investors worry that its cloud business isn't growing as much as rivals'. Amazon stock closed 1.7% higher at $234.11 Thursday ahead of results. Shares are down 7.7% at $216.02 Friday premarket. Sales at Amazon Web Services, the group's cloud-computing arm, grew 17.5% on year to $30.87 billion in the second quarter. That wasn't enough for investors. "Competition remains fierce for cloud computing services and Amazon's sales growth rate is struggling to match rivals Microsoft and Alphabet," AJ Bell's Dan Coatsworth writes in a note. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

0826 GMT - Shares in insurer AXA fall as much as 7% before paring back some losses after the stronger euro ate into the French group's earnings. The stock is the worst performer of the Stoxx 600 insurance index. Keefe, Bruyette & Woods analyst William Hawkins notes that the headline earnings miss against consensus stands out and is primarily driven by foreign exchange. "We should know this already. It is more prominent in our minds as a 5% headwind to future earnings growth than we thought previously," he writes in a research note. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0822 GMT - The reaction on markets to U.S. tariffs on trade partners such as Canada and Switzerland has been relatively muted due to the fact that trade agreements have been sealed with Japan and the European Union, IG analysts say in a note. "Markets were relatively calm as key deals with Japan and the EU were already secured, and talks with China and Mexico continue," they say. The pan European Stoxx 600 index declines 1.1% to 539.84 points. Switzerland's SMI index falls 0.8%. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0819 GMT - Samsung Electronics could report a strong rebound in 3Q operating profit on better sales and profitability from its memory-chip segment, Nomura analysts led by C.W. Chung write in a research note. The South Korean technology giant's operating profit is forecast to jump 87% on quarter for the July-September period, they reckon. The company's high-bandwidth-memory shipments is forecast to surge 90% on quarter in 3Q, following a 28% rise in 2Q. DRAM prices are expected to rise by more than 10% on quarter in 3Q, they add. Nomura expects Samsung's return on equity to rise to 11.4% in 2026 from 7.6% in 2025. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

0816 GMT - Apple shares rise premarket after the iPhone maker reported better-than-expected sales for its fiscal third quarter. Apple stock closed 0.7% lower at $207.57 Thursday ahead of results. Shares are up 2% at $211.79 Friday premarket. The tech giant reported $94.04 billion in sales for the three months ended June 28, above a FactSet forecast of $89.60 billion. The iPhone accounted for the lion's share of sales, contributing $44.58 billion. Analysts polled by FactSet had forecast $40.05 billion in iPhone sales. Net profit of $23.43 billion also beat consensus of $21.67 billion. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

0802 GMT - Oil prices are up significantly on week, with Brent and WTI 6% and 6.3% higher, respectively. With a better growth backdrop from positive economic data releases and concerns over tightening Russian supply in July, Brent crude posted its best month since September 2023, Deutsche Bank analysts say in a note. Oil prices rallied late in the month after President Trump threatened new sanctions or secondary tariffs against importers of Russian oil, with a deadline of Aug. 8, analysts write. U.S. trade policy also appeared more successful over July, with a raft of trade deals signed late in the month, raising economic hopes and optimism for oil demand. In early morning trade, Brent crude is flat at $71.69 a barrel and WTI is down 0.1% at $69.22 a barrel. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

0802 GMT - IAG's 250 million-euro EBIT beat in 2Q is enough to drive upgrades of 5% to 6%, RBC Capital Markets analyst Ruairi Cullinane says in a note. The airline group lowered its total fuel cost guidance for the year by 5.3% to 7.1 billion euros, which could make consensus estimates rise by even more, he says. The company is 57% booked for the second half with revenue in line with last year, compared with 29% booked for the 2H last year. "We think IAG has a stronger outlook than given credit for, given a supportive capacity backdrop on the Atlantic, pricing potential from BA product measures, and given scope for business travel recovery to step up further," he says. Shares are up 1.3% at 385.50 pence. (anthony.orunagoriainoff@dowjones.com)

0758 GMT - Macau's gaming industry is likely to maintain solid momentum, according to Citi analysts in a research note. Gross gaming revenue in July beat Citi's estimates and market consensus. Looking ahead, Eason Chan's concert series at the Galaxy Arena over the next two weekends is likely to boost gross gaming revenue similarly to Jacky Cheung's in June and July, Citi says. Citi raises its August gross gaming revenue forecast to 21.5 billion Macanese Pataca from 20.0 billion Macanese Pataca, implying on-year growth of 9%. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)

0750 GMT - The dollar should gain further if U.S. jobs data for July is strong but any rise is likely to be limited, MUFG's Derek Halpenny says in a note. The data is due at 1230 GMT. Good news on the U.S. economy is already largely reflected in the price of the dollar, which rose by 3.19% against a basket of currencies in July, its best month since April 2022. "The [dollar's] strong gain in July does suggest positive news is better priced, which will contain the scope for dollar strength today," he says. Still, a bigger-than-expected rise in nonfarm payrolls could remove the prospect of an interest-cut in September, he says. The DXY dollar index edges up 0.1% to 100.022, having hit a two-month high of 100.145 earlier. (jessica.fleetham@wsj.com)
